canada_day.intro: Canada Day on 1 July celebrates the 1867 confederation that united the colonies into the Dominion of Canada.
canada_day.history: On 1 July 1867 the British North America Act joined Ontario, Quebec, New Brunswick and Nova Scotia into one dominion. Originally ‚Dominion Day‘, it was renamed Canada Day in 1982, the year Canada gained full control over its constitution.
canada_day.traditions: Celebrations centre on Parliament Hill in Ottawa with concerts and fireworks. Across the country people wear red and white, fly the maple-leaf flag, and gather for parades, barbecues and citizenship ceremonies.
funFacts: It was called Dominion Day until 1982.
The maple-leaf flag was only adopted in 1965.
Many new citizens are sworn in on Canada Day.

remembrance_day.intro: Remembrance Day on 11 November honours Canadians who served and died in war, symbolised by the red poppy.
remembrance_day.history: 11 November recalls the 1918 armistice that ended the First World War. Canada, which lost more than 60,000 in that war, honours the fallen of all conflicts. The poppy comes from the poem ‚In Flanders Fields‘ by Canadian John McCrae.
remembrance_day.traditions: At 11 a.m. Canadians observe two minutes of silence. The national ceremony at the National War Memorial in Ottawa features wreaths, the Last Post and a flypast, and millions wear poppies in the weeks before.
funFacts: ‚In Flanders Fields‘ was written by Canadian John McCrae in 1915.
Poppies are worn from late October until 11 November.
The Silver Cross Mother lays a wreath for all bereaved families.
